content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

May 12, 1992

Isabelle Lemon
Manager, KNBR Promotion
55 Hawthorne Street, Ste. 1100
San Francisco, CA  94105-3914

Re:  Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-232

Dear Ms. Lemon:

You have requested advice on behalf of KNBR concerning the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/

QUESTION

What disclosure obligations, if any, has KNBR incurred in
connection with expenditures made to support Measure G on the San
Jose ballot?

CONCLUSION

KNBR must file campaign disclosure reports if it makes
contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year to
California state or local candidates or committees, or if it makes
independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar
year to support or oppose California state or local candidates or
ballot measures.

FACTS

KNBR is the flagship station for Giants' baseball in Northern
California. The station traditionally posts a billboard campaign
every March and this year posted 210 billboards. After the

1/     Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800  •  P.O. Box 807  •  Sacramento CA 95804-0807  •  (916) 322-5660

Isabelle Lemon
Our File No. A-92-232
Page 2

billboards went up. KNBR decided to add a "snipe" to each of the
85 billboards placed in the San Jose area supporting the ballpark
initiative in San Jose (Measure G). The snipe says "yes on G" and
comprises approximately one-eighth of the total board. Although
the San Jose Ballpark Committee was aware of KNBR's actions prior
to adding the snipe, the decision to add the snipe was made by
KNBR and was not made in concert with the committee.

ANALYSIS

Entities which make contributions or expenditures in
connection with California state or local elections must file
periodic campaign disclosure reports if they meet the definition
of "committee" under the Act. An entity or organization which
does not receive contributions from others will qualify as a
"committee" if it:

...(b) Makes independent expenditures totaling
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more in a calendar
year; or
(c) Makes contributions totaling ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) or more in a calendar year to or
at the behest of candidates or committees....

Section 82013.

Section 82031 defines "independent expenditure" to mean a
payment for a communication which expressly advocates the
nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified candidate,
or the qualification, passage or defeat of a clearly identified
measure, but the payment is not made directly to--or at the behest
of--the affected candidate or ballot measure committee. "Made at
the behest" means that the payment was made under the control or
at the direction of, in cooperation, consultation, coordination,
or concert with, or at the request or suggestion of the affected
candidate or committee. (Regulation 18215(b).)

Your letter indicates that in your opinion, KNBR's
expenditure was not made at the behest of the San Jose Ballpark
Committee. Under these circumstances, KNBR has made "independent
expenditures" to support Measure G and will be required to file
campaign disclosure reports as an independent expenditure
committee if the cost of producing the snipes and adding them to
each billboard totalled $1,000 or more. (Section 82013(b).)

However, if you determine that the expenditure was made at
the behest of the San Jose Ballpark Committee, the fair market
value of the activity was a contribution to the Committee. The
fair market value is whatever it would have cost the Committee to
purchase the same amount of advertising on the open market. Under
these circumstances, KNBR will be required to file campaign
disclosure reports only if it qualifies as a major donor committee

Isabelle Lemon
Our File No. A-92-232
Page 3


(i.e., all of its contributions to state and local candidates and
committees total $10,000 or more during the calendar year).
(Section 82013(c).)

     Enclosed are the appropriate campaign disclosure forms and an
information manual.  Please contact me at (916) 322-5662 if you
have questions concerning this letter.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature: Carla Wardlow]
                                        By:  Carla Wardlow
                                             Acting Division Chief
                                             Technical Assistance and
                                             Analysis Division

Enclosures

KNBR must file campaign disclosure reports if it makes
contributions totaling $10,000 or more in a calendar year to
California state or local candidates or committees,
or if it makes
independent expenditures totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar
year to support or oppose California state or local candidates or
ballot measures.

CONCLUSION: KNBR must file campaign disclosure reports if it makes
